This thing has the news clipping show. This created mass panic. People were fleeing their homes in every direction. Now of course we lived at a time, remember when we had consensus media. So probably a fourth of all homes that had a radio were tuned in to this broadcast. In today's fragmented media, 12 people'd hear it and some people would get it off of, you know, Tivo two days later when they were on their treadmills. And the monsters would get everybody. But I mean the brilliance of this, like the authenticity of the eyewitness, instead of being perfectly scripted, he sounds exactly like the doofus eyewitnesses that are interviewed on the news now, right? I mean it's like perfect. All things was perfect. And fear happens internally to stimulus. And so this thing built and built and built until people were taken action. They're running, they're fleeing. Kevin Robert who was in the burial insurance business made his first fortune there. Kevin always said you can't sell burial insurance unless they see a hearse parked out in the driveway. And that's really what the fear button is all about. They have to see it and they have to feel it. So when you do it from the front of the room, when you do it in copy, when you do it with pictures, you're going for a very closed eyes, internal result is what you're trying for. I mean people have to, so like I have a story for a client of mine, they didn't want to use it. But it is a act now, because who knows how much time you got left story. And it's a big long story about a guy who's starting out on a normal day and he has all these plans and the family is all getting together. And then of course he has a heart attack and he drops dead and none of this happens. It's a wonderful life in reverse story is what it is. But it has to build, see it has to build. There's this great, I didn't want to take a half an hour to show it, I've shown it I think once. There's a, for years in the fire alarm business there was a film used called Another Man's Family. And it's what they showed when they went in the house to sell fire alarms. And it is painfully slow, like when you sit here and watch it. But it is this build of this, of course it was shot in the 50s, it was used all the way through the late 70s. And so it's, you know, the Donna Reed household and dad is going off to work in the morning and mom is gonna bake cookies and little Timmy and the dog are gonna go play out in the yard. And it's this ever slow build up of kind of normalcy all through the day into the evening when dad returns home from work. And the dog is all through it, right? And so you don't really, you don't think anything of it. But the dog is an integral part of this whole setup which is like 20 minutes of the 30 minutes all the way to bedtime, right? And they've laid, Timmy and the dog have been on the floor while the family has watched TV. Now it's time to go to bed, yada, yada, yada. It's Timmy and a girl, forgive me. It's Timmy and, you know, Cindy or whatever. So there's two kids and there's the dog and there's mom and dad, right? And now everybody goes to bed. So there's this long, slow build. And now the fire, you know, the electrical cords, they got 4,000 things plugged into one outlet behind the couch and boom, off it goes. Then the drapes are on fire and then the couch is on fire and then the whole house is on fire. And you now leap to after it's all over and the firefighters are there and the whole place has burned to the ground and you know, everybody's dead and there's charred wood laying around and the fire guys are walking through the charred wood and oh, here's the dog. And the dog is with the little doll that the girl has been playing with all through the half hour with its paws over the doll. And the camera slowly goes into the dog and a tear trickles out of the corner of the dog's eye and runs down the side of its face, right? And everybody buys alarms. That's the deal, right? Because it scares the shit out of them, right? And it does it at this methodical pace but they are genuinely afraid now to go to sleep. If they don't buy alarms, you can bet your ass. They're going all over the house that night looking behind the couch, they're unplugging shit. We don't really need this to be plugged in until tomorrow. I mean, the biggest problem in that business was now you had to install, you know, they didn't want to go to bed without alarms but the installer comes three days later and puts the alarms in. That was the biggest problem in that business. But that sort of fear building, and then if you tie a hope to it, then that's very, very powerful. 55 is certainty. God knows people want this. So, I assume probably everybody has. It doesn't hurt you to reread it every once in a while. It's thin. But Eric Hoffer's book, True Believer, is one of the great how to run a movement text. And he's very clear and some thinking about what works and what doesn't will tell you he's right that the effectiveness and influence of a doctrine does not come from the meaning but from its certitude. It's not effective unless it is presented as the one and only truth. Every diet has to be presented. So if it's the Paleo diet, is that how you say it? It's the Paleo diet. All other diets have to be bullshit. This has to be the only one. You can't sell it effectively by saying it's a good one. There's lots of good diets. You could do weight watchers, you could do neutrosystems, you can go on the Paleo diet, you can go on the Mediterranean diet. It's really sort of a matter of personal choice. There's lots of different roads that lead to Rome. There's lots of different ways to be right. You're not gonna sell nothing. Because that is not what people want. They want to be told the one right answer. My friend Herb True is business professor, no, no, no, he's too early every year. He would give the class five books to go read on like a management theory. And they would all come back and wanna know, okay, Prof, which one's the right book? It's sorta like this morning's question from you, right? It's okay, you're giving us 57 of these things. Tell me the three that really matter, right? We all want certitude. We want an answer, not a portfolio of answers. We want an answer. We want the diagram, the chart. I've always thought 10 was probably too many. The commandments, but it seems like we could have combined a few of those and got them down to six or something. You know, I'm not sure, but particularly when you consider they were writing them on stone tablets, a little abbreviation might've been helpful. But so people want this one answer and they want doctrine and they want a rigid doctrine. Whether it's about diet or fitness or money making, how you do a listing presentation in real estate, how you do anything. They want a one way that is the right way to do X. And they are most influenced by the person or institution that is most dogmatic and rigid about the one way to do X. And the temptation to deviate from this in all sorts of businesses, including ours, is enormous because the flexibility in order to use the big tent theory, that idea seems to make so much sense on its surface. Oh, in order to attract a lot more people to whatever, Republican parties as good an example as any, and make the tent bigger, we need to be more, now put in a bunch of words, less rigid, more flexible, more tolerant, more whatever in order to let more people come into the tent. The irony is that the more of that you do, the less people come into the tent. That's the irony, because that's really not what they're looking for. So if you look at the Democrat side in this election in the primaries, she has barely survived a 72 year old socialist. He's not even a Democrat, and he's running in the Democratic Party for the Democratic nomination. Like, I ain't even a Democrat. All they had to do was say, no, you can't run. And he wouldn't have even been there. But they thought he wasn't gonna matter. It would be fun for a few months, and it would keep everybody interested, and then he'll go away, right? So he's a 72 year old socialist, he's a lunatic. Nice guy, I'm sure, he seems to be a nice guy. I think he'd be fun to sit around and argue with. I think it'd be fun. But, you know, he's crazy. Stark Raven nuts. And there's been very little scrutiny of him because nobody thinks he's mattered, but there's some shit in his background that, I mean, he's batshit crazy, this guy. And he's 72 years old, and he's a socialist. And she's barely survived him. How can this be? It's easy. His positions are certain and absolute. And therefore, exclusionary, too many, while hers are vague and fuzzy, and she is trying to straddle and be flexible, and five different answers to the same question. The only, this I gotta see. This, who is this? This I gotta see. See, not, see, not, I almost would have bet on Sydney. I almost would have bet on Sydney. I would have, you know, the brusque New Yorker, I don't give a shit, I'm gonna eat when I wanna eat. You know, and here it is, well, you do know everybody in your row gets a bite. So you take a bite and you pass it down the row. This is, you know. I'm so into that. I'm not. What'd you get? I'm vegetarian, but they're bringing me the extra meal that I'm supposed to bring you that's right there, but they got to eat now. I'm not. So what'd you get? Oh, nice, good. Huh? Got a bib? You don't wanna, you don't wanna, you don't wanna spell any of that on you. Did you get a wine? It's a Shave Dare, Shave Dare pasta without wine. You want us to get you a glass of wine? I'll send somebody. You know, I don't want this to be tough for you. So, yeah, obviously. That we could fix. So people are looking, so the only Republicans that have gotten elected in our lifetime to the presidency, except for old man Bush, who inherited it, have violated the whole Big Ted idea. Reagan, Nixon, George W., all absolutists, very rigid, far to the right. Everything being said about Trump, by the way, was said about Reagan every single day, right? Oh my God, we can't have this guy with his thumb on the button, you know, same stuff. So there's this tendency with every business and every movement, every, oh, if we just kind of water it down and we make it okay to be, I mean, I'm fascinated. I hate offending folks who are, but I am fascinated with the Catholic religion. You've gotta be fascinated with this. I mean, I get what he's doing. I understand the pressure, right? Because our numbers are dropping, right? So we're gonna liberalize, we're gonna loosen up all the rules and that'll work better. I don't know how to break the news of this guy, but he's making a big marketing mistake. This is not gonna work. This is a bad idea. I mean, when he said a few weeks ago, oh, maybe a month and a half ago now, I can almost do it verbatim. He said, assuming that they translated him correctly, to be fair, but he said, in matters of family, marriage and sex, you pay attention, you get me, catch me if I'm wrong. People should rely on or use or listen to their own conscience. What are this? And I'm like, Carl is a lapsed Catholic. Well, if you count her twice, I've been married to three of them. I run into him and say, you gotta commit here. You are not gonna believe what he's done now, right? You are not gonna believe this. I mean, it's one thing, okay, you don't have to eat fish anymore, right? It's another thing, St. Christopher metal forget it. But like, this is a big deal. This is doctrine now, you're kind of on your own, right? Birth control, whatever you want, it's okay. Now, I'm not arguing the position, I'm arguing the marketing mistake, right? Which is the idea is if we loosen this up, more people will come. No, less people will come, because that's not what they want. They want a way, the right way. That's what they want. They wanna be able to fight over who has the right way in everything. That's why we have war. People wanna fight over who has the right answer to a question. The idea is that we never have a religious based war. If everybody took the position that they're all right. Oh yeah, I'm a Catholic and I think Buddhism is equally correct. We're all gonna meet up in the same place and play golf together in the afterlife. There gonna be wearing funny robes and we're gonna be wearing funny hats, but it's all okay, everything's the same. There'll be nobody in any church. There wouldn't be any wars, but there wouldn't be anybody in any church. Everybody wants the way. Atkins who conquered the diet industry for a decade did it by there being only one right way, mine. And everything else is nonsense and charlatanism. And when you see people watering it, it's because they falsely think that flexibility equals attraction. That's what they think. So I've told the story before, so the first chiropractor, I went to three chiropractors, never stuck with any of them. And I've had back trouble on my line. And so the fourth one I go to, 1980, 81 maybe, same, they all did the same thing. How many of you been to a chiropractor? Okay, so you all know the drill. They examined the report, they put the thing up on a light box. If your whole spine's about to fall out, and if you don't, you're gonna be crawling around on all fours. So you need to come in 52 times a week for the first 12 weeks, and then you need to come in 18 times a week for the next 16 weeks. Ultimately you wind up in maintenance for life twice a week. That's the whole, and they all did the same shit. Just to script this stuff. So she does the same, and you gotta go through it, right? If you go to a new doc, they make you get blood tests. You gotta do it. So you gotta, I got the x-rays, no, no, no, no, no. So you gotta go through this thing. So okay, fine. So you gotta come in, and we're gonna preschedule your first X number of appointments. So I've let her sell. I mean, I've really been very courteous. But now it's my turn to sell. And I am always a better salesperson than anybody who's selling to me. So now I'm selling my story, right? Which is that's all fine for all your doofus patients. But you know, I'm an important and busy guy, and I'm on airplanes all the time. I can't be book and shit six weeks ahead because big clients might show up. I'm getting to, my plan is, I'll call you in the morning when I wanna come in. See, that's my plan. So I get it that you got your plan, but I have my plan, right? And I'm gonna sell my plan. And when I get done, she says, excuse me, she comes back with a phone book. And she says, ch-ch-ch-character. Here, I'm gonna circle three of them. They'll all take you on that basis. And they're all very good doctors, and they'll probably do a fine job for you. But we can't have that here in our practice. That's not what we do here. We have a contract, we have a goal. There's certain things I have to do. There's certain things you have to do. You have to enter into this agreement. So let me give you these pages out of the phone book and go on your merry way, right? All right, wait a minute. All right, so now I'm signed, right? Now, they all, probably all still do this. Probably give it to you as an app now, I forbid. But all chiropractors used to now send you home with the exercise sheet. And they're old drawings of the exercises you're gonna do on both sides. And they've checked off the ones you're supposed to do, and they've written in a number of reps, right? So you're supposed to do this one 10 times, and you're supposed to do this one eight times. You don't have to do this one, and you couldn't. But you gotta do this one three times, et cetera. And here's your exercise sheet, right? Which mine gets conveniently stored under the seat of the car. So I am now back for an adjustment, which is what I wanted in the first day in place, right? And so, in she comes to the treatment room, and she says, are you doing your home exercises? You bet, right? Wouldn't miss them for a minute, right? So excited about that, I'm doing them in the morning, it's hard to sleep. You know, I'm doing them. All right, great, show me number six. Fuck. Fuck. Fuck. Fuck. Sorry, no adjustment, go home, come back for your next one. If you still aren't doing them, then we're back to the three pages out of the yellow pages plan, because you can't be here if you're not gonna do your exercises. Now, she kept me for four years, the only reason I stopped going to her is because I moved, right? Because she actually had certainty. Here's what has to be done for us to get the result we want. And this is it, this is the plan. And no, you can't have your plan, you have to have my plan, because I'm the doctor and you're the patient. This is what most people want. They are literally, as Kavitz said, rather cynically, they are walking around with their umbilical cord in one hand, looking for a new place to plug it in. That's what they are. And the winners give it to them. So I gave you on page 80, you now actually, enough, I think you have it now, actually in the real newsletter, but I gave it to you. And there's other things here, so there's use of the familiar, there's analogy, there's story, there's some other things you can find in here, but this is basically an example of a lecture of certainty. This is a lecture of, there's one way, there's only one way, and no, there ain't no flexibility. And it has the added virtue of truth, but regardless of that, this is what really wins, is this kind of position. So, we are at five minutes of three, we will take our break, which puts us at three 10. We'll start at three 10 and we will give away
